content.text: What is On-Call & Runbooks?
On-call rotations and runbooks are the operational backbone of reliable production systems. An
on-call rotation
is a schedule that designates which engineer is responsible for responding to production incidents outside of normal business hours. A
runbook
is a documented procedure that tells that engineer exactly what to do when a specific type of incident occurs — what to check, what commands to run, how to escalate, and how to resolve the problem.

For AI agent systems, the stakes are higher than for conventional web services. Agents can consume cloud credits at an alarming rate when looping, make irreversible tool calls (sending emails, writing database records, calling third-party APIs), exhibit subtle safety failures that compound quietly, or stall mid-task in ways that cause cascading downstream failures. The combination of LLM non-determinism, external tool dependencies, long-running async workflows, and significant per-invocation cost makes AI agents among the most complex systems to operate in production — and therefore among the most in need of mature on-call and runbook practices.
Runbooks are not just checklists. A well-authored runbook explains the
why

behind each step, includes decision trees for ambiguous situations, specifies rollback procedures, and links to the monitoring dashboards and logs needed to diagnose the problem. They are living documents that improve with each incident.
Why It Matters

Without on-call structure and runbooks, incident response is improvised. Every on-call engineer independently rediscovers the same diagnostic steps, makes different decisions under pressure, and spends precious minutes reconstructing context that was already known. Mean Time to Resolve (MTTR) is longer than it needs to be, and repeat incidents happen because lessons from previous incidents are never codified.
For AI agent systems specifically, several failure patterns demand structured response procedures:
Cost runaway: an agent enters a retry loop or spawns unbounded subagents, burning thousands of LLM tokens per minute until a spending limit is hit or a human intervenes.

Safety policy breach: the agent produces output that violates content policy, sends a harmful action to a downstream tool, or is manipulated by prompt injection. These incidents require both immediate containment and careful audit of affected conversations.

LLM provider degradation: an upstream model provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.) experiences latency spikes or partial outages. Agents time out, users see stalled sessions, and queues fill up. The runbook needs a fallback procedure: switch model, reduce context size, or queue for retry.

Tool integration failure: a tool the agent depends on (web search, code execution sandbox, CRM API) goes down. Agents that cannot gracefully degrade start hallucinating results or returning incomplete outputs.

Memory or state corruption: long-running agents accumulate context that grows beyond model limits, leading to truncation errors or incoherent responses. Stateful agents backed by a database can also encounter data races if multiple agent instances write to the same conversation record.

Each of these failure types has a distinct diagnosis path and mitigation strategy. Runbooks encode that knowledge so any on-call engineer can resolve the incident confidently, even if they have never seen this class of failure before.
Benefits
Reduced MTTR: a clear set of diagnostic steps eliminates time wasted on exploratory investigation. An engineer with a runbook resolves incidents in minutes rather than hours.

Distributed ownership: runbooks allow any team member to handle incidents in any part of the system, not just the original author. This is critical for sustainable on-call rotations with reasonable rotation sizes.
Institutional memory: runbooks capture knowledge that would otherwise live only in the heads of the most experienced engineers. When those engineers leave, the knowledge stays.
Consistency: every on-call engineer follows the same steps, producing consistent decisions and outcomes. Auditors and compliance teams can verify that responses meet regulatory requirements.

Improved alert quality: writing runbooks forces the team to think carefully about what alerts are actionable and what they mean. Alerts without runbooks often reveal alert coverage gaps or poorly calibrated thresholds.
Continuous improvement: runbooks that are updated after every incident form an improvement loop. Each incident makes the next one faster to resolve.
Trade-offs & Limitations

Maintenance burden: runbooks rot. Commands that worked six months ago now fail because the infrastructure changed. A runbook that engineers do not trust is worse than no runbook — it adds a false confidence layer. Someone must own keeping runbooks current.

Cannot cover every scenario: novel failure modes — especially those caused by LLM behavior changes after a model upgrade — will not be in the runbook. Engineers need judgment in addition to procedure.
On-call fatigue: a rotation that pages too frequently burns out engineers. High-volume low-quality alerts are more damaging than no on-call rotation at all. Alert thresholds need tuning.

False sense of security: having runbooks does not mean incidents are handled well if the runbooks themselves are wrong or incomplete. Runbooks must be tested — either through tabletop exercises or by explicitly running through them during low-stakes incidents.
Time investment: writing good runbooks takes time that feels expensive before an incident happens. Teams that skip runbook authoring almost always regret it during their first major outage.
How It Works

The on-call and runbook system is a closed loop: alerts fire when something breaks, engineers are paged, runbooks guide resolution, and post-incident reviews update both the runbooks and the alert thresholds.
The lifecycle starts with
alert definition

. An alert is defined by three things: a metric or log query that detects the problem, a threshold that distinguishes normal from abnormal, and a severity classification (P1 through P3 is common). For AI agents, relevant metrics include error rate per agent run, average and p99 LLM response latency, total token spend rate, tool call success rate, and safety filter trigger rate.
When an alert fires, the
alert routing

layer classifies it, deduplicates it against open incidents, and pages the on-call engineer. Routing tools like PagerDuty and OpsGenie implement escalation policies: if the primary on-call does not acknowledge within a defined window, the alert escalates to a secondary, then to a manager.
The on-call engineer acknowledges the alert, opens the linked runbook, and follows its steps. Steps typically progress from
triage
(is this actually broken, and how bad is it?) through
diagnosis
(what is the root cause?) to
mitigation
(stop the bleeding) and then
resolution

(fix the underlying cause). Mitigation and resolution are deliberately separated — the priority during an incident is to reduce user impact quickly, even if that means rolling back a change or disabling a feature. A permanent fix can follow in a calmer state.
After the incident is resolved, a
post-incident review (PIR)

— sometimes called a post-mortem — is held within 24–48 hours while the incident is fresh. The PIR produces an incident timeline, a root cause analysis, and a set of action items (alert improvements, code fixes, runbook updates). Critically, PIRs are
blameless
: the goal is to understand how the system failed, not to assign fault to an individual. Blame-oriented reviews cause engineers to hide information and reduce the quality of the analysis.
Architecture Overview

attrs.alt: On-call runbook architecture: alert sources feed into alert router, which pages on-call engineer and triggers runbook lookup, leading to remediation steps and post-incident review

content.text: When to Use It

On-call rotations and runbooks are appropriate for any AI agent system that is depended on by users or business processes outside of normal business hours. If an agent failure at 2am on a Saturday causes user impact or business loss, someone needs to be reachable — and that person needs documented procedures.
Introduce on-call rotation when:
The system handles user traffic 24/7 and failures have immediate user impact.
The system runs long-duration autonomous tasks (hours or days) that can fail partway through in ways that require human intervention.
The system can incur significant cost if left in a broken or looping state overnight.
Regulatory or contractual SLAs require availability guarantees that demand active monitoring and rapid response.

Start authoring runbooks earlier than feels necessary — before the first production deployment, not after the first major incident. Each alert you add to your monitoring system should have a corresponding runbook written at the same time. If you cannot write the runbook, the alert is not ready for production.
Do
not

establish a 24/7 on-call rotation if all failures can be safely deferred until business hours. A cost anomaly alert that fires at 3am for a batch processing job that runs overnight might be better handled by automatic rate-limiting or spending caps than by waking an engineer. Only put humans on-call for failure modes that genuinely require human judgment to resolve quickly.
How to Implement
Implementing a production-ready on-call and runbook system involves five interconnected steps.
Define your alert taxonomy.

Identify every failure mode for your AI agent system and classify each by severity (P1/P2/P3), expected frequency, and required response time. P1 alerts wake someone up immediately. P2 alerts notify during business hours or interrupt after a delay. P3 alerts are filed as tickets for the next sprint.
Instrument your agents.

Add structured logging and metrics to every agent execution. Emit events for: task start/end, LLM API calls (latency, token counts, error codes), tool calls (success/failure, latency), memory operations, and safety filter triggers. Feed these into a time-series database (Prometheus, DataDog) with dashboards and alerting rules.
Configure the paging chain.

Set up PagerDuty or OpsGenie with your on-call rotation schedule, escalation policies, and acknowledgement windows. Link each alert to its runbook URL so the on-call engineer can navigate directly to the procedure from the page.
Author runbooks.
Write one runbook per alert. Use a consistent template (see below). Store runbooks in Git under a
runbooks/

directory in your main repository, or in a dedicated runbooks repository. Every runbook must include: alert description, expected frequency, impact, diagnostic steps, mitigation steps, escalation contacts, and related dashboards.
Establish a post-incident review process.
After every P1 or P2 incident, schedule a blameless review within 48 hours. Track action items in your issue tracker. Assign owners and deadlines. Follow up to confirm action items are completed.
Examples
Runbook template structure (Python-rendered Markdown)

import textwrap
from datetime import datetime

def generate_runbook_template(alert_name: str, service: str, severity: str) -> str:
    """Generate a standardised runbook Markdown template."""
    return textwrap.dedent(f"""\
        # Runbook: {alert_name}

        **Service:** {service}  
        **Severity:** {severity}  
        **Last updated:** {datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}  
        **Owner:** #agent-oncall  

        ## What fired
        <!-- Describe in plain English what the alert means and why it fires. -->

        ## Impact
        <!-- Describe user-visible impact and business impact. -->

        ## Triage (< 5 minutes)
        1. Open the [Agent Error Dashboard](https://grafana.internal/d/agent-errors)
        2. Check the service health endpoint: `curl https://api.example.com/health`
        3. Check for upstream LLM provider incidents at https://status.anthropic.com

        ## Diagnosis
        ```bash
        # Fetch the last 50 error log lines for this service
        kubectl logs -l app={service} --tail=50 | grep ERROR

        # Check current token spend rate (tokens/minute)
        kubectl exec -it deploy/{service} -- python -c \
            "from app.metrics import get_token_rate; print(get_token_rate())"
        ```

        ## Mitigation
        - **If cost runaway:** disable the agent task queue:
          `kubectl scale deploy/{service}-worker --replicas=0`
        - **If LLM timeout:** switch to fallback model via feature flag:
          `kubectl set env deploy/{service} LLM_FALLBACK_ENABLED=true`
        - **If tool failure:** enable degraded mode:
          `kubectl set env deploy/{service} TOOL_DEGRADED_MODE=true`

        ## Resolution
        <!-- Describe how to fully restore normal operation. -->

        ## Escalation
        - Primary: on-call engineer (PagerDuty)
        - Secondary: engineering manager
        - P1 after 30 min: VP Engineering

        ## Related runbooks
        - [LLM Timeout Spike](./llm-timeout-spike.md)
        - [Cost Anomaly Response](./cost-anomaly-response.md)
    """)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    template = generate_runbook_template(
        alert_name="Agent High Error Rate",
        service="research-agent",
        severity="P1",
    )
    with open("runbooks/agent-high-error-rate.md", "w") as f:
        f.write(template)
    print("Runbook generated.")
Programmatic alert registration with alert-to-runbook linking

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class Severity(str, Enum):
    P1 = "P1"  # immediate page, 5 min ack SLA
    P2 = "P2"  # page with delay, 30 min ack SLA
    P3 = "P3"  # ticket, next business day


@dataclass
class AlertDefinition:
    name: str
    severity: Severity
    runbook_url: str
    description: str
    # Prometheus-style PromQL expression
    query: str
    # Threshold that triggers the alert (units depend on the metric)
    threshold: float
    for_duration: str = "5m"  # must be true for this long before firing
    labels: dict = field(default_factory=dict)


# Agent-specific alert definitions
AGENT_ALERTS = [
    AlertDefinition(
        name="AgentHighErrorRate",
        severity=Severity.P1,
        runbook_url="https://runbooks.internal/agent-high-error-rate",
        description="More than 10% of agent runs are failing with errors.",
        query='rate(agent_run_total{status="error"}[5m]) / rate(agent_run_total[5m])',
        threshold=0.10,
        for_duration="5m",
        labels={"team": "agent-platform"},
    ),
    AlertDefinition(
        name="LLMLatencyHigh",
        severity=Severity.P2,
        runbook_url="https://runbooks.internal/llm-timeout-spike",
        description="Median LLM response latency has exceeded 10 seconds.",
        query='histogram_quantile(0.50, rate(llm_latency_seconds_bucket[5m]))',
        threshold=10.0,
        for_duration="10m",
        labels={"team": "agent-platform"},
    ),
    AlertDefinition(
        name="AgentTokenSpendAnomaly",
        severity=Severity.P1,
        runbook_url="https://runbooks.internal/cost-anomaly-response",
        description="Token spend rate is 5x above the 7-day rolling average (possible loop).",
        query=(
            'rate(llm_tokens_total[5m]) > 5 * '
            'avg_over_time(rate(llm_tokens_total[5m])[7d:5m])'
        ),
        threshold=1.0,  # the query itself is boolean; threshold is > 0
        for_duration="3m",
        labels={"team": "agent-platform", "cost": "true"},
    ),
    AlertDefinition(
        name="SafetyFilterTriggered",
        severity=Severity.P1,
        runbook_url="https://runbooks.internal/safety-policy-breach",
        description="Safety filter has triggered more than 3 times in 10 minutes.",
        query='increase(safety_filter_trigger_total[10m])',
        threshold=3.0,
        for_duration="0m",  # fire immediately
        labels={"team": "agent-platform", "safety": "true"},
    ),
]


def render_prometheus_rules(alerts: list[AlertDefinition]) -> str:
    """Render alert definitions as a Prometheus alerting rules file."""
    rules = []
    for a in alerts:
        rule = (
            f"  - alert: {a.name}\n"
            f"    expr: {a.query} > {a.threshold}\n"
            f"    for: {a.for_duration}\n"
            f"    labels:\n"
            f"      severity: {a.severity.value}\n"
        )
        for k, v in a.labels.items():
            rule += f"      {k}: \"{v}\"\n"
        rule += (
            f"    annotations:\n"
            f"      summary: \"{a.description}\"\n"
            f"      runbook_url: \"{a.runbook_url}\"\n"
        )
        rules.append(rule)
    return "groups:\n- name: agent_alerts\n  rules:\n" + "\n".join(rules)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(render_prometheus_rules(AGENT_ALERTS))
Real-World Example

Consider a production research agent that runs overnight literature reviews for a legal team. The agent fetches academic papers, calls an LLM to extract relevant passages, and writes structured summaries to a database. The team has three engineers on a weekly rotation.

At 2:17am, a PagerDuty alert fires: "AgentTokenSpendAnomaly — token spend is 8x the 7-day average." The on-call engineer receives a phone call. She opens her laptop, acknowledges the alert within 3 minutes (within the P1 SLA), and opens the linked runbook.

The runbook's triage step says to check the agent dashboard and look for runs that have been active for more than 60 minutes (normal runs complete in 5–15 minutes). She finds 12 agent runs all stuck on the same paper — it has 400 pages and the LLM is being called once per paragraph.
The runbook's mitigation step for "loop or unbounded iteration" says to scale the worker to zero immediately, then cancel all active runs via the task queue. The engineer runs:

# 1. Stop accepting new tasks
kubectl scale deploy/research-agent-worker --replicas=0

# 2. Cancel all active agent runs
python scripts/cancel_active_runs.py --older-than-minutes 60 --dry-run
# Dry run output: Would cancel 12 runs (run IDs: ra-1042, ra-1043, ...)

python scripts/cancel_active_runs.py --older-than-minutes 60
# Output: Cancelled 12 runs.

# 3. Verify token spend has dropped to zero
kubectl exec -it deploy/research-agent-api -- \
  python -c "from app.metrics import get_token_rate; print(get_token_rate())"
# Output: 0 tokens/min

# 4. Scale worker back up
kubectl scale deploy/research-agent-worker --replicas=3

Total time from alert to mitigation: 14 minutes. The engineer files a PIR and goes back to sleep. The next morning, the team adds a per-run token budget guard and a maximum-pages-per-document limit to the agent code, and updates the runbook to include this class of failure.
Common Mistakes
Alert fatigue from noisy alerts.

If an alert fires multiple times per week without action, engineers learn to ignore it. Every alert must be actionable — if you cannot write a runbook for it, you should not ship it. Review alert firing rates monthly and eliminate or tune alerts with low signal-to-noise ratios.
Runbooks that are never tested.

A runbook with stale kubectl commands, outdated service names, or missing environment variables is worse than no runbook — it adds confusion under pressure. Run through each runbook at least once per quarter, even if only as a tabletop review against a staging environment.
Skipping the mitigation/resolution distinction.

Runbooks that mix mitigation (stopping the bleeding) and root-cause resolution lead engineers to attempt complex fixes at 2am under stress. Keep mitigation steps simple and reversible. Root-cause fixes happen later, in daylight, with full context.
No spending circuit breakers for agents.

Without hard per-run token budgets or LLM provider spending alerts, a looping agent can rack up thousands of dollars in API costs before any human notices. Set provider-level spending alerts (Anthropic, OpenAI dashboards both support this) as a backstop independent of your own monitoring.
Blameful post-incident reviews.

A review that results in blaming the engineer who deployed a breaking change will cause engineers to avoid deploying on Fridays (cargo-culting) and to hide relevant context during future reviews. Psychological safety in PIRs is not optional — it directly determines the quality of information you extract from incidents.
Missing escalation paths for novel AI failures.

AI agent failures sometimes require domain expertise that the on-call engineer does not have — a subtle safety failure may need an ML safety engineer, a model behavior change may need someone who understands the model's fine-tuning history. Define escalation contacts for AI-specific incident types and include them in runbooks.
Best Practices
One runbook per alert: every alert that fires should link directly to the runbook that handles it. Do not use a single generic runbook for multiple alert types.
Write runbooks to the lowest-context audience: assume the on-call engineer has never seen this part of the system. Link to documentation, dashboards, and code. Do not rely on tribal knowledge.
Store runbooks in version control: runbooks in Git are reviewable via pull request, diffable over time, and auditable. Do not store them in a wiki that only one person knows how to edit.

Add a runbook-last-updated timestamp and rotate staleness review: any runbook older than 90 days without a review should be flagged for verification. Automation can check modification dates and file tickets.

Include copy-pasteable commands: runbook steps that require shell commands should provide the exact command, not a description. A stressed engineer should not be constructing kubectl commands from scratch.

Set explicit per-run budgets in agent code: the runbook for cost runaway is much simpler if the agent itself has a hard token budget that triggers a clean shutdown rather than requiring external intervention.

Track PIR action items to completion: a PIR that produces action items nobody follows up on is worse than no PIR. Use a dedicated label in your issue tracker (e.g., pir-action-item) and review open items in weekly engineering standups.
Measure and publish on-call health metrics: share MTTD, MTTA, MTTR, pages per week, and PIR action item completion rate with the whole team. Visibility drives improvement.
Performance & Scalability

On-call systems have their own performance constraints. For small teams (two to five engineers), a single shared rotation is feasible. As the team and system grow, the rotation should be restructured:
Sub-team rotations: separate rotation pools for infrastructure, agent platform, and model quality avoid paging engineers for incidents outside their area of expertise.
Follow-the-sun: with globally distributed teams, divide the on-call shift so each region covers its own daytime hours. No engineer is regularly woken up at 2am.

Alert volume management: keep pages per on-call shift below 5 for sustainable operations. Google's SRE book recommends a maximum of 2 pages per 12-hour shift. Above this threshold, alert thresholds need tuning or more automation is needed.

For runbook scalability, structured runbook formats (YAML or JSON alongside Markdown) allow programmatic generation, validation, and linking. A runbook registry that maps alert names to runbook URLs can be queried by your alert routing layer to automatically inject the runbook link into every page.
Tooling & Ecosystem

Category
Key capabilities
Notes
PagerDuty
On-call routing
Rotations, escalation policies, runbook links in alerts, mobile app
Industry standard; higher cost

On-call routing
Rotations, escalation, Jira integration, free tier available
Good for Atlassian shops

Alerting rules
PromQL alert definitions, deduplication, grouping, silence

Observability + alerting
Metrics, logs, traces, LLM Observability add-on for AI agents

On-call dashboard embedding, alert annotations, Grafana OnCall

Best practice: treat runbooks like code
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
How it handles incidents
Strengths
Weaknesses
On-call rotation + runbooks (this topic)
Engineer paged; follows documented procedure
Handles novel failures; human judgment available
Human latency; rotation fatigue; requires maintenance
Auto-remediation only
Alert triggers automated fix script; no human involved
Fastest possible response; zero sleep disruption
Cannot handle novel failures; automation bugs can amplify incidents
Business-hours support only
Failures wait until 9am; no paging
No engineer fatigue; lowest operational cost
Unacceptable SLA for 24/7 systems; cost runaway persists overnight
Hybrid: auto-remediation + runbooks
Automation handles known classes; human handles the rest
Reduces page volume; faster mitigation for known failures
More complex to build and maintain; two systems to keep in sync
Chaos engineering + runbooks
Proactively injects failures in staging to test runbooks
Validates runbooks before real incidents; builds confidence
Higher engineering investment; overkill for early-stage systems
Security & Reliability

Runbooks and the tooling that executes them are high-value targets. A compromise of your runbook library can introduce malicious mitigation steps. A compromise of your on-call paging system can suppress incident notifications during an attack. Several controls are important:

Require pull request review for runbook changes: treat runbook modifications like production code changes. A bad mitigation step that scales a deployment to zero (instead of scaling it down) can cause an outage.

Separate on-call tooling credentials from production secrets: the scripts run by on-call engineers should have the minimum privileges needed — read-only access to logs, specific kubectl verbs (scale, set env), not cluster-admin. Use Kubernetes RBAC to enforce this.

Audit logs for mitigation actions: every kubectl command and every configuration change executed during an incident should be logged with the engineer's identity and timestamp. These logs are essential for PIRs and compliance audits.

Redundant paging channels: if PagerDuty or OpsGenie goes down during an incident, you need a backup notification channel (email, SMS via a different provider, Slack with a dedicated #oncall channel). Test the backup channel quarterly.

Protect safety-related runbooks: runbooks for safety policy breaches may contain information about how your safety filters work. Restrict access to these documents to need-to-know — do not publish them in a publicly readable wiki.

Test escalation paths annually: simulate a P1 incident during business hours to verify that the paging chain, escalation policy, and communication channels all work correctly. Update contact information and test notifications after every personnel change.
Key Takeaways

On-call rotations and runbooks are not optional for production AI agent systems — their failure modes (cost runaway, safety breaches, tool failures) require structured, timely human response that improvised incident handling cannot reliably provide.

Every alert must link directly to a runbook that tells the on-call engineer exactly what to check, what commands to run, how to mitigate, and when to escalate. If you cannot write the runbook, the alert is not ready for production.

Keep mitigation separate from resolution: the goal during an active incident is to stop user impact quickly using simple, reversible steps. Root-cause fixes happen later, with full context and in a calm state.
Blameless post-incident reviews are the primary mechanism for improving both runbooks and system reliability over time. Blame-oriented reviews suppress information and slow down learning.
Runbooks rot: schedule quarterly reviews, treat runbook PRs like production code PRs, and update runbooks immediately after every incident that reveals a gap or a stale step.

AI agent systems need agent-specific alert types that conventional web services do not require: per-run token budgets, LLM provider latency tracking, tool call success rates, and safety filter trigger counts.

The hybrid approach — automating mitigation for well-understood failure classes while maintaining runbooks for everything else — produces the best balance of response speed and human judgment as a system matures.
